coredata.dc:description: Supercapacitors are highly attractive energy storage devices that produce enormous quantity of energy and high-power density which can be useful to fulfil the ever increasing demand for energy worldwide. In this respect, a new type of carbonaceous material, known as graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have wide ranging applications in supercapacitor field given its exceptional physicochemical properties, high surface area and abundant edge sites for modification. GQDs are zero-dimensional (0D) carbon nanomaterial, derived from a two-dimensional (2D) graphene, by altering the 2D graphene sheets into 0D GQDs. These exhibit new properties due to quantum confinement and edge effect, which is almost similar to that found in graphene. These materials find numerous applications in the field of sensing, bioimaging, catalysis, supercapacitor, adsorption, and separation. In this paper, the doping and modification techniques of GQDs, including fabrication of energy storage devices as well as their significant applications in the field of supercapacitors and deficiency of GQDs as energy conversion material and issues around its storage capacity. We also present possible research avenues and opportunities, as well as highlight associated challenges.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Supercapacitors are highly attractive energy storage devices that produce enormous quantity of energy and high-power density which can be useful to fulfil the ever increasing demand for energy worldwide. In this respect, a new type of carbonaceous material, known as graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have wide ranging applications in supercapacitor field given its exceptional physicochemical properties, high surface area and abundant edge sites for modification. GQDs are zero-dimensional (0D) carbon nanomaterial, derived from a two-dimensional (2D) graphene, by altering the 2D graphene sheets into 0D GQDs. These exhibit new properties due to quantum confinement and edge effect, which is almost similar to that found in graphene. These materials find numerous applications in the field of sensing, bioimaging, catalysis, supercapacitor, adsorption, and separation. In this paper, the doping and modification techniques of GQDs, including fabrication of energy storage devices as well as their significant applications in the field of supercapacitors and deficiency of GQDs as energy conversion material and issues around its storage capacity. We also present possible research avenues and opportunities, as well as highlight associated challenges.

ce:section.ce:para: The production of energy and its consumption has had much impact on the world economy and environment, particularly, with a growing demand for a long-life, eco-friendly and high-performance renewable energy storage systems [1]. In this respect, batteries have become accepted as one of the most favourable technology over the past years as these are considered useful in supplying necessary energy and required power density [2,3]. However, due to their i) low energy conversion efficiency, ii) insufficient power density supply and iii) limited energy storage capacity, a new type of energy storage system is crucially needed. In this respect, after an intensive investigation, a novel kind of energy storage device is reported, known as electrochemical capacitors or supercapacitors [4–9]. Supercapacitors are devices that can supply high energy and power density to fulfil the demand for energy and are known as a cost-effective and environmentally friendly energy conversion and storage devices [7]. As an alternate to diminishing fossil fuel supplies, which causes environmental pollution over large scale; smart materials like supercapacitors are gaining significant attention for the development of such new materials. Moreover, the energy storage capacity, the energy storage capacity, its performance, and reducing environmental concerns are also some essential aspects of this research. Incidentally, the increasing demand for supercapacitors is due to high specific capacitance, long life cycle, high power density, fast charging/discharging rate, and low or almost free maintenance cost. These devices have several applications such as portable power devices and electronic vehicles that need quick charging-discharging capacity [10–13].

GQDs are 0D carbon-based nanomaterial which exhibits the extraordinary properties akin to graphene. The lateral dimension of GQDs is <10 nm and thickness <2 nm which means there are a number of applications of it in the field of nanomaterials and fabrication of devices [14]. Furthermore, GQDs have good biocompatibility, large surface area, high π-π conjugate bonding, better surface modification, excellent chemical inertness, and non-toxicity properties [15,16], and can be used to provide energy in rural areas where no commercial facilities are available. Given these properties and the rapid research and development in the energy storage technology, supercapacitors are becoming an alternative to the future commercial battery for high energy density values [17–21].

Supercapacitors are a type of electrochemical storage devices that behave like a bridge and fulfil the gap between batteries and capacitors. Several metal oxides (such as MnO2, RuO2, and NiO); metal nanoparticles, carbonaceous materials (e.g. graphene oxide (GO)); reduced graphene oxide (rGO); GQDs; carbon nanotube (CNTs); and carbon fibers; conducting polymers (e.g. polypyrrole, polyaniline, and polythiophene) are mainly utilised as super-apacitive materials [22–29]. These materials are capable of accommodating high energy storage density, high specific capacitance, and large charge-discharge rate.

Though the transition metal compounds are excellent capacitive materials, it is hard to use them alone due to their poor electrical conductivity, slow performance rate, structural instability, and reduced life cycle. amongst these materials, the carbonaceous materials exhibit an excellent charging-discharging rate in excess of 500,000 cycles [30,31]. Particularly, the GQDs have been extensively employed in energy storage applications for their attractive electrochemical properties, excellent thermal as well as chemical stability, and good electrical conductivity [16,19,32–34]. GQDs contain both sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon atoms with several functional groups like amino, carbonyl, epoxide, and hydroxyl groups at the surface, which acts as a reaction site. The GQDs have typical quantum confinement and edge effects with lateral dimension <10 nm and thickness less than (<2 nm) making it as an excellent candidate in the field of nano-devices fabrication [31]. Furthermore, due to extensive π-π conjugation, the surface area and surface grafting property of GQDs is much higher than graphene. GQDs can be easily manipulated by the assembly, functionalization, controlled modification, and stacking prevention [35]. It has been shown using the Density functional theory (DFT) that the band gap of GQDs is about 7.4 eV [36]. Therefore, the GQDs itself can be used as supercapacitive material in an energy storage system.

In order to enhance the surface area, increase the cycle stability, and improve the conductivity, several metal oxides, nanoparticles, and sometimes conductive polymers are incorporated with GQDs (Scheme 1 ). These types of materials can eliminate the stacking and produce the species which is electrochemically active. These active species participate in reversible redox reactions through charging-discharging processes, which increase the specific capacitance [37]. Therefore, the functionalization or modification with GQDs exhibits unique properties and a wide range of applications in high-performance capacitance study. This review is mainly focused on two aspects of supercapacitor-based research; i.e. on the properties and application of GQDs as supercapacitor; and secondly, on the design aspect of supercapacitor which is based on modified GQDs. We have considered materials incorporated with GQDs and can be used as electrode. In the design aspect, we are mainly concerned with electrochemical performance and the results were applied as supercapacitors. Additionally, several synthetic procedures, essential properties, challenges, and future possibilities are also discussed.

Supercapacitors, as an energy storage system, are receiving increasing interest in the research field due to their tiny size, high power density, quick charging-discharging properties, and long life. Many materials have been employed in developing supercapacitor devices [38,39]. Like, CeO2 mixed LaMnO3 nanocomposites were used as electrode material for supercapacitor device fabrication [40]. LaMnO3 acts as an electrode material due to its high ion-adsorption and superior electrochemical energy storage capability. Furthermore, to enhance the conductivity and specific capacitance of LaMnO3 nanocomposites, Ce3+ is doped. These nanomaterials manifest high specific capacitance of 262 F g −1 at a specific current of 1 A g − 1. Another material reported by Robinson et al. is based on additive-free Co3-xMnxO4 nanoparticles for supercapacitor electrode fabrication [41]. These transition metal oxides exhibit excellent specific capacitance, fast Faradaic redox activity, and long cyclic stability due to their stable crystal structure. Such materials show a specific capacitance of 173.6 F g − 1.

Though these materials are used in supercapacitive performance, they suffer from low volumetric capacitance, difficulty in synthesis, agglomeration, and are expensive for commercial production. Thus, the synthesis of composite materials that combine with carbonaceous nanomaterials (GQDs) may solve such problems. These are zero-dimensional materials with the exclusive properties of both quantum dots and graphenes [42]. GQDs have impressive response in both the medical and sensing fields for its exceptional electrochemical and physicochemical properties like large surface area, outstanding electrical conductivity, and good dispersion in aqueous and various solvents [43,44]. Several articles have already been published on GQD-based nanomaterials as supercapacitive material. For instance, Chen et al. reported 3-D GQDs based composites for supercapacitor applications. The composite material was synthesized by electrochemical deposition of GQDs on 3D graphene (GQD-3DG). These materials have a large surface area, long life cycle, and high charging-discharging capacity demonstrate significant capacitance property with a specific capacitance of 268 F g −1 at a current density of 1.25 A g −1 [45]. Another research group, Liu et al., reported the GQDs//PANI asymmetric micro-supercapacitor by two-step deposition process [46], resulting into a fast power response capability and long cyclic stability. These materials showed magnificent capacitive performance with 534.7μF cm−1 [23] and extraordinary properties make GQDs excellent nanomaterials which combine with various materials, thereby developing conducting substances for supercapacitive performance. Materials like this provide porous and layered architecture for the transport of electrons and electrolyte ions, while GQDs can increase the electrical resistance and specific capacitance of composites materials.

A zero-dimensional graphene derivative GQD with tiny lamellar size (<10 nm), are considered as promising precursors to develop high-performance supercapacitors, for the following reason; (1) the zero-dimensional conjugated carbon skeleton is much flexible for developing novel and conductive architectures; (2) the various functional groups and enriched edge architecture may offer a large number of active sites for the fabrication of energy storage device; and (3) the excellent migration property and electrochemical reactivity allow their operation in a very flexible way [47,48].

Based on the characteristics of GQDs, Jia et al. developed porous carbon nanosheets with hierarchical architecture (HPCNs) by using GQDs (prepared from coal) for supercapacitor electrode fabrication [49]. It was synthesized from the bituminous coal by simple liquid-phase oxidation. Due to presence of a functional group at the edge, enriched edge structure, small size, good stability, and good flexibility, these GQDs are suitable to develop the nano-device for energy storage purposes. These HPCNs showed an interconnected graphene-like network with a surface area 1332 m2 g −1, orderly distributed pores, excellent conductivity, more active sites, and sufficient channels for ion migration. The specific capacitance of this nano-device is 230 F g −1 (at current density 1A g −1), and the retention of initial capacitance was 74% after 1000 cycles. This work provides a new direction to design and development of carbon-based advanced nanodevice for an energy storage system.

Another work is reported by Tong et al., in which, a unique synthesis method has been established where GQDs grafted onto carbon fibre (CF) directly by the in-situ process through covalent ester linkages at 90 °C [50]. The manufacturing of flexible supercapacitor by using the grafted technique showed higher electrochemical capacitance performance (around 5.5 times) than that of CF. The grafting technique provides more active sites on the GQDs surface, creating defects and preventing GQD recombination. These retained around 97% of its initial capacitance approximately after 5000 cycles at current density 2 A g −1. The stability of the supercapacitor proved its potential in the field of advanced electronic devices fabrication.

In the recent years, toughing materials are preferred for supercapacitor electrode fabrication. Generally, the toughing materials are small-dimensional structures (e.g. 0D, 1D), which provide high electrochemical activity and cycling stability. It can elongate the crack propagation path thereby escaping the sudden destruction of molecular structure. GQDs are 0D carbon nanomaterials, which have strong quantum confinement, high electrical conductivity, and good stability, which are considered as the ideal toughening materials [51,52]. Based on this, Yuan et al. developed hybrid material based on NiCo-layered double hydroxides (two-dimensional (2D) (LDHs)) and GQDs [53]. In this hybrid material, the high specific capacitance is provided by NiCo-LDHs. NiCo based LDHs have typically a layered structure, high surface area, a fast ion transport channel, high theoretical capacitance (~2220 F g −1), excellent redox activity, and low cost. To enhance the electrochemical performance and long cyclic stability, GQDs were introduced in the above hybrid material, which acted as a toughening agent. With under 1 A g-1 current density, these hybrid materials show high specific capacitance of 2220 F g-1, and high cycling stability.

It is well known that the size of GQDs shows a significant role in high-performance device fabrication. Usually, the size of the GQDs is less than 100 nm. In this regard, Yu et al. synthesized GQDs from graphene oxide (GO) and nitric acid treatment by top-down strategy [34]. The synthesized GQDs had a uniform size ≤ 5 nm. The edge effect and small size of GQDs creates a greater number of the active sites as well as improved the π-π conjugated electronic structure. These electrode materials had a high specific capacitance of 296.7 F g −1 at current density 1 A g −1 with excellent cycling stability, less relaxation time, and low internal resistance.

Recently, GQDs were also used to fabricate carbon nanofiber fabrics (CNFs) for the development of supercapacitor electrodes. CNFs are a type of nanofibre that contains nanofiber morphology along exceptional structural and functional integrity. CNFs have a porous structure with a large surface area, good electrical conductivity, and high stability, which provides a strong and continuous conductive network in the whole system. Motivated by this phenomenon, Fan et al. reported GQDs, which uniformly embedded in CNFs, had a high surface area 2032 m2 g −1 as well as high electrical conductivity [55]. This electrode showed significant capacitance of 335 F g −1 at 1 A g −1 as well as high retentions power of capacitance of 77% and 45% at 100 A g −1 and 500 A g −1, respectively.

Another way to increase the performance of supercapacitors is by loading of high mass carbon in the electrode. The thick electrode reduced electrical conductivity by blocking the ion migration channels. Based on this phenomenon, Lin et al. developed a high mass loading carbon electrode for advanced supercapacitors performance [56]. The conductive porous carbon was prepared from flexible GQDs by micelle-induced assembly technique. The conductivity of GQDs was comparatively higher than the commercially activated carbon due to a large number of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms. The interconnected mesoporous architecture of GQDs stimulates the ion transporting property, especially at a high mass loadings condition. It showed specific capacitances of 315 and 170 F g −1 at current density 1 and 100 A g −1, respectively.

Generally, doping is an effective method to incorporate some elements in GQDs to modulating their electrical, optical, and structural properties. There are two primary conditions for doping, including, i) the size of the element which is to be doped should be of the same size exactly as that of the atom present in the crystal, and ii) after incorporation of dopants, there should be no distortion in the crystal. Based on these criteria, various dopants are used to enhance the conductivity of GQDs. Such dopant can enhance the electrochemical performance of GQDs by enhanced Faradaic interaction between electrolyte ions and GQDs surface; and can also increase ions transporting channel width as well as improve the electronic conductivity. Thus, the doping of heteroatom is a very productive method for modulating the electronic structure of GQDs [17]. In GQDs based supercapacitors, the carbon is moderately replaced with few elements such as Boron (B), Phosphorous (P), Iodine (I), Nitrogen (N), Selenium (Se), Sulfur (S), and Silicon (Si) [57,58]. These replacements alter the character of GQDs, as an example, doping with the Group 16 element (S and Se) enhances the absorption property of GQDs [57,58].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of mechanism: alteration of supercapacitor property by coordination with doping materials
ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, researchers have reported that the doping of heteroatom is a very efficient method for modulating the characteristics of GQDs. The incorporation of heteroatom alters the band gap from lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO, π) to highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO, π*) level [59]. The electronic architecture of graphene layers can be improved by doping with electron-donating and accepting units. The incorporation of heteroatoms develop the chemical as well as physical properties of GQDs and most notable characteristic observed in GQDs, is adjusting the electronic band-gap that enhances the mechanical strength, electrical conductivity and longevity. The heteroatoms (non-metallic), including B, N, S, and other atoms, can effectively replace the C (the main element of GQDs) and thus forming electron- holes by migration of electronic cloud. These influence doped GQDs to show excellent role in catalysts, electrochemical capacitors, and fuel cells, etc. [59]. amongst the elements, the doping of Nitrogen is a simple, easy and, effective way to enhance supercapacitive performance. Nitrogen, having a similar atomic size to carbon, is incorporated into the graphene matrix and forms covalent bonds. It increases the Faradaic interaction between carbon surface and an electrolyte ion during the charging/discharging processes, enhances the conductivity of carbon materials, and improves the surface wettability of electrolyte [60–64]. Motivated by the above phenomenon, in 2017, Li et al. developed nitrogen-doped rGO and GQDs composites (rGO/GQDs) to prepare high-performance supercapacitors [37]. The nitrogen doping enhanced the binding energy of GQDs and GO sheets and improved the electrical conductivity (Fig. 2 ). The supercapacitive performance of NrGO/GQDs electrodes was higher (344 F g −1 at a current density of 0.25 A g-1) compared to rGO and low nitrogen-doped rGO electrodes. Furthermore, the NrGO/GQDs electrodes exhibited the initial capacitance 82% after 3000 cycles (current density of 0.8 A g −1) in 6 M KOH electrolyte.

Recently, Kharangarh et al. reported another supercapacitive material, which was developed by co-doping iron and sulphur atoms with GQDs using the hydrothermal treatment [65]. To enhance electrochemical performance, its life cycle, mechanical strength, and charging/discharging rate, iron (II) sulphur were doped in GQDs. In this work, iron and sulfur oxide were selected as dopant materials due to its appropriate voltage window and high specific capacitance. It showed a high reversible redox reaction with an excellent specific capacitance of 476.2 F g −1 compared to that of GQDs which is 143F g −1. Additionally, it also increased the electrical conductivity and electron transfer property.

Wu et al. reported nitrogen-doped GQDs/graphene hydrogel/ carbon fibers (N-GQD/GH/ CF) for high-performance supercapacitors [66]. In this material, GH was developed on CFs surface to form a porous 3-dimensional network (GH/CF). After that nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) were deposited electronically on the GH/CF network. The synthetic procedure of N-GQD/GH/ CF is shown in Fig. 3 .

Finally, the N-GQDs were deposited electrically onto GH/CF network and prepared N-GQD/GH/ CF. The experimental cyclic votammetry (CV) curves showed that the supercapacitive response of N-GQD/GH/CF was much higher than that of N-GQD/GH and GH/CF (Fig. 4 a). Fig. 4b shows the CV curves of N-GQD/GH/CF at different scan rates. The results indicate that, within the electrode, N-GQD/GH/CFs have a quick response on reversing the voltage and have almost negligible resistance on ion transport. Fig. 4c showed the fast charging-discharging curves (at 0–0.8 V, at a current density of 40 mA cm−3). Fig. 4d showed the charge/discharging graph of the N-GQD/GH/CF FFSC (at different current densities) which is almost in symmetrical shape. The volumetric capacitances were calculated from galvanic charging-discharging curves and were plotted in Fig. 4e. At 20 mA cm−3 current density, the specific volumetric capacitance of N-GQD/GH/CFs is 93.7 F cm−3, which was comparatively higher than N-GQD/GH and GH/CF. The extraordinary activity of N-GQDs was shown due to the reversible redox reactions between pyrrolic-N/pyridone-N and oxygen-containing groups. Moreover, in porous N-GQD/GH/CFs, N-GQDs also increased electrolyte wettability and enhanced the ion-reachable surface area. In N-GQD/GH/CF composite material, CF and GH increase the surface area and mechanical adhesion with N-GQDs.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) study was performed, and Nyquist plots of GH/CF, N-GQD/CF, and N-GQD/GH/CF FFSC are presented in Fig. 4f. The result indicated that the material has high electrical conductivity with good electron transport and ion diffusion pathways. Fig. 4g shows the high energy density (2.1 mWh cm−3, at a power density of 8 mWh cm−3) of N-GQD/GH/CF FFSC. The N-GQD/GH/CF based device maintained its initial capacitance to 87.9% after 5000 cycles (Fig. 4h). Generally, the synergistic effects from the GH, CF, and N-GQDs component-based N-GQD/GH/CF electrode exhibited high specific capacitance.

Recently, Zhang et al. reported histidine-functionalized GQDs/Ni-Co LDH (His-GQD/LDH) composite for the development of supercapacitor electrodes [67]. Here, histidine is selected for nitrogen doping purpose, which increases the electrical conductivity as well as the electron transport property. These composite materials were synthesized by microwave method. The electronic surface area and conductivity of composite material increases the synergistic effect of His-GQD and LDH resultant the composite shows high specific capacitance (1526 F g −1) and excellent cycling stability. The characteristic of supercapacitor are are shown in Fig. 5 . Fig. 5a shows the CV curve at various scan rates. The curve is looking like a leaf, which indicated the fusion of electric double layer and faradaic capacitance. The specific capacitances of device are 138, 127, 92, 88, 85 and 80 F g −1 which was calculating from the galvanostatic charge/ discharge (GCD) curve, shown in Fig. 5b. Ragone plot of device is displayed in Fig. 5c which shown that device possesses energy density of 48.89 Wh kg−1 at 0.80 kW kg−1. The inset images of 5c showing that various LED are lighted by this supercapacitor successfully. Fig. 5d showing the cycling stability of device. After 6000 cycle, the device showed 91.13% of its initial capacitance which indicate the extraordinary performance of the device.

Mahyari et al. developed GQDs based polyaniline (PANI)/metal oxide (CeO2) to form active electrode materials for a hybrid supercapacitor (PANI-N,S-GQDs@CeO2) [68]. In these composite materials, the highly π-conjugated PANI was introduced for its high specific capacitance and low cost. However, this compound has low mechanical stability and low cyclic stability. Furthermore, CeO2 nanoparticles were also incorporated to enhance active surface area and contact area. However, poor conductivity and agglomeration of CeO2 nanoparticles have limited its application. To avoid such problems and to further enhance the contact area, N and S co-doped-GQDs were introduced. The supercapacitor properties of the PANI-N,S-GQDs@CeO2 were investigated by CV, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, and galvanostatic charging/discharging experiments. The highest specific capacity 189 Cg−1 was obtained from PANI/5 wt% N,SGQDs@ CeO2 nanocomposite as compared to 175 and 115 Cg−1 from PANI/10 wt% N,S-GQDs@ CeO2 and PANI/5 wt% CeO2 respectively at a current density of 1 Ag−1. PANI/5 wt% N,SGQDs@ CeO2 device retains 75% of its initial capacitance after 1000 cycles.

Some researchers used fluorine as a dopant along with nitrogen [69]. In carbon dots, the strong electron-withdrawing element, fluorine, and nitrogen atom can reduce the HOMO and LUMO energy levels [69,70]. The lowered LUMO level of fluorine and nitrogen dopant is similar with actual HOMO level of GQDs. As a result, at original domains in the GQDs and the interfacial junction of dopant, the HOMO level of GQDs induces a hole in LUMO, which again combined with electrons present in HOMO. This combination produces suitable electron-hole pairs at GQDs-water interfaces, which is useful to enhance the electrocatalytic activity. For example, Sim et al. prepared fluorine and nitrogen-doped GQDs (N,F-GQDs) for water splitting and supercapacitor device fabrication [70]. Owing to the significant difference of electronegativity between F and C, it is used as a doping material for the fabrication of supercapacitive electrode material. The supercapacitive material showed specific capacitance of 270 F g −1 at 1 mV s −1, and galvanostatic charging-discharging (GCD) capacitance of 244 F g −1 at 3 mA cm−2.

The conducting polymer like Polyaniline (PANI), polystyrene sulfonate, Polythiophene (PTh), Polypyrrole (PPy), and Poly (3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) can offer high conductivity and excellent supercapacitor activity [71–74]. However, the use of the above-conducting polymer alone is not appropriate due to mechanically unstable film formation. Furthermore, the cyclic stability of polymer-based electrodes is comparatively lower than carbon electrodes. Though, the above polymer can boost conductivity, it also shows a volume change due to swelling, which enhances the thickness of the electrodes. Thus, to avoid such problems and to increase the stability and current density, these materials are incorporated with GQDs, which provide suitable supercapacitive material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of mechanism: alteration of supercapacitors property by coordination with conducting polymer
ce:section.ce:para: The combinations of conducting polymers with GQDs are showing comparatively higher conductivity, higher energy density, and specific capacitance. When the conducting polymers are incorporated with GQDs, it increases the electroactive surface area, which improves the electrochemical property and current flow. In this regard, Wang et al. designed PANI/GQD-rGO/CFC (carbon fibre cloth) electrodes by spraying technique to develop solid-state supercapacitor [75]. The different layer of PANI was subsequently coated as well as self-assembled on the surface of GQD-rGO-modified CFC. By repeating this self-assembly process by spraying techniques the multilayer of PANI/GQD-rGO was obtained on the CFC surface (Fig. 6 ).

In this work, carboxy modified GQDs was selected for good amphilicity. The homogeneously dispersed rGO solution was prepared by using GQDs. Here, GQDs acted as a surfactant. The resultant GQDs-rGO solution was treated on the CFC surface by the solution spraying method. It is observed that as a result, GQDs-rGO provides the hydrophilicity in CFC, whereas CFC and PANI bear a high level of conductivity. The conductive PANI layer was self-assembled on the surface of GQD-rGO-modified CFC by the spraying technique. Thus, by repeating the spraying process, multilayers of PANI/GQD-rGO were assembled on CFC surface. This layer-by-layer (LBL) spraying technique modified the hydrophobic CFC surface to a hydrophilic surface effectively and improved the interaction between CFC and PANI without affecting the basic skeleton of CFC. In this way, a flexible as well as highly stable, high-performance CFC capacitor was developed by employing the H2SO4/PVA gel, which behaves as both binder and electrolyte. This constructive capacitor showed maximum capacitance power of 82.9 mF cm−2 (1036 F g −1) at a 0.1 mA cm−2 current density. It showed excellent stability and 97.7% retention after 10,000 charging-discharging processes. These self-assembly LBL spraying techniques indicate great benefits, including easy operation, lower cost, good controllability, and versatility. It can be utilized for the development of wearable and portable photoelectronic devices.

A nanofiber was prepared using a coating of polyvinyl alcohol-GQDs-cobalt oxide (PVA-GQD-Co3O4) composite with poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) (PEDOT) by electrospinning and electropolymerization technique [76]. To develop these nanofiberes, PEDOTs have been selected because it is one of the well-known π-conjugated conducting polymers. It shows excellent stability in its oxidation state. The polymerization of PEDOTs is performed in both organic and aqueous solutions. Here, PVA was used as a polymer precursor that provides unique properties such as cost-effective, excellent mechanical strength, high biocompatibility, non-toxic, high solubility, and significant electrospin ability [77]. This material was incorporated with Co3O4, which has a high theoretical capacitance, excellent redox activity, and non-toxic property induced by electrospun technique. The above nanofiber composite showed a high specific capacitance of 361.97 F g −1 and excellent stability with 96% retention after 1000 charging-discharging cycles. Furthermore, it also showed high specific energy and excellent specific power ranged from 16.51 to 19.98 Wh kg−1 and 496.10–2396.99 W kg−1 (at 1.0 to 5.0 A g −1current density).

Mofrad et al. reported ferrocenyl modified GQDs (GQD-Fc) for supercapacitor applications. To improve the electrode conductivity, PPy was mixed with the GQD-Fc and a nanocomposite was obtained [75]. Due to the presence of iron and nitrogen-rich contents with a large surface area, the synthesized nanocomposite showed excellent capacitance behaviour [78]. The specific capacitance of 284.01 F g −1 (at current density 2.5 A g −1) with 86% retention of capacitance after 5000 charging-discharging cycles along with the outstanding energy density 81.79 Wh kg−1 at power density 18.17 kW kg−1.

In another study, Madhuri et al. developed GQDs doped polyacrylonitrile (PAN) and PANI composite (PAN-PANI@G) for preparing high-performance supercapacitors [78]. The structure of PAN-PANI@G nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 7 . In this composite material PANI was introduced with GQDs for higher conductivity and high current density. However, PANI is not able to form a stable film. To provide mechanical strength in PANI based GQDs, PAN was introduced in the composite nanomaterials. PAN is well-known organic polymer resins which have good thermal, chemical, and mechanical stability [77,79,80]. It is an efficient binding agent for polymers and makes it easy for the synthesis of the nanomaterials. The charged PAN-PANI@G based supercapacitor is shown in Fig. 8 ; displaying 1.4 ± 0.1 V of power supply up to 60 min of working time. This result indicates the significant supercapacitor performance of the PAN/PANI@G (1.5 wt%) which can be used in fabrication of low cost, simple and power saving supercapacitor/storage devices. The material showed high specific capacitance was 105–587 F g −1 cm−2 at current density 670 mA g −1.
The comparison between GQDs and its composite as Supercapacitive material are shown in Table 1 .

There are further studies where significant effort has been made on incorporation of various nanoparticles with GQDs to increase the energy storage capability. For instance, the metal oxide nanoparticles, which are mainly involved in bonding with GQDs are MnO2, CuCo2S4, and MoS2. These nanomaterials are shown to provide a specific surface area and abundant porosity for the rapid exchange of ions and electrons [81,82]. As an example, the composite of the GQD/MnO2 supercapacitor electrode was fabricated by in-situ formation of GQDs on the MnO2 nanosheet surface by plasma enhanced chemical vapour deposition (PECVD) technique. Under the current density 1 A g −1, the above material shows high specific capacitance 1170 F g −1. The high electrochemical performance of GQD/MnO2 composite occurred due to the high specific surface area of MnO2 nanosheets and excellent interaction with conductive GQDs [83]. Thus, the combination of nanoparticles with GQDs acts as the electrode material for supercapacitor performance due to the high specific surface area, high electrochemical activity, and good coupling and low cost of nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of mechanism: alteration of supercapacitor property by coordination with nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: The composite between GQDs and nanomaterials with a large surface area acts as the supercapacitive electrode material. It provides excellent electrochemical and chemical stability, high electrical conductivity, and is a low cost material. These materials exhibit rectangular-shaped CV curves and the symmetrical charge-discharge galvanostatic profile. These factors influence the electrochemical performance by increasing specific surface area, by modifying the shape as well as the distribution of pores, functionality of the surface, and electrical conductivity. The combinations of GQDs with binary metal nanomaterials including, NiCo2S4 and NiCo2O4 are ideal supercapacitive material due to high electrochemical performance and high conductivity [84,85]. These nanomaterials contain two different metal ions, which increase the rate of redox reactions and maintain higher electrochemical performance. Based on this phenomenon, Mu et al. prepared a tremella-like NiCo2O4@GQDs composite where the NiCo2O4 is encapsulated by GQDs homogeneously [86]. Herein, the π-conjugated core-based GQDs with NiCo2O4 were suitable for advanced electric energy storage (EES). The incorporation of NiCo2O4 on GQDs surface increased the conductive network, which enhanced the electrochemical performance of NiCo2O4@GQDs composite material significantly. Furthermore, the porosity of tremella-like NiCo2O4 enhances the wettability between the electrolyte and material, which gave long cycle stability of the composite materials. Thus, the synergistic effect acted between tremella-like NiCo2O4 and the conductive GQDs. The synthesized NiCo2O4@GQDs composite reveals an excellent specific capacitance (1242 F g −1) at current density 30 A g −1, which was higher than that of pristine NiCo2O4 (790 F g −1 at current density 30 A g −1) in 2.0 M KOH electrolytes.

In the composite material, the combination of two different metal cations improved the electrical as well as ionic conductivity and capacity because of the synergistic effect of the two distinct components. Thus, the combination of two different metal cations showed active electrochemical performance. However, the uses of metal component alone lower the electrical conductivity, and the intrinsic defects, which decrease the rate of reaction and cycling stability [87–89] [87–90]. To overcome these problems, researchers have focused on the combination of transition metals with GQDs to improve the specific capacitance, enhanced cyclic stability, and provide more active sites. In this respect, Tang et al. developed novel nanocomposites based on GQD-doped CuCo2S4 by the hydrothermal process for the fabrication of supercapacitor electrodes [90]. After the combination of GQDs with CuCo2S4 nanosheets, the surface of nanocomposite changed from smooth to particle type [Fig. 9 ]. As a result, electrochemical cycling process, charge transfer kinetics, and ion diffusion capability of GQD/CuCo2S4 improved for supercapacitor performance. The result demonstrated that the specific capacitance of GQD/CuCo2S4 was 1725 F g −1 at 0.5 A g −1 current density with high cyclic stability.

Another work reported by Tang et al. is based on GQDs-mediated hierarchical-hollow NiCo2S4 nanostructures under hydrothermal condition [85]. They showed that the internal architecture and surface morphology of NiCo2S4 have been altered after the introduction of GQDs. The unique nanostructures of GQDs based NiCo2S4 have better electrochemical performance, high surface area, extra interspace, pathways for both ion diffusion, and high specific capacitance (678.22 F g −1 at the current density of 0.2 A g −1) than bare NiCo2S4. The cycling stability of GQDs based NiCo2S4 is much higher than the bare one and retained its initial capacitance (almost 94%) after 5000 cycles.

Sangpour et al. have reported GQDs-MoS2 nanocomposite for high capacitance supercapacitor performance [91]. In this work, they have used Molybdenum disulfide (MoS2). The MoS2 is one of the transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs), which have a graphite sheet-like layered structure [92]. Incidentally, MoS2 has drawn considerable attention in various fields, like, catalyst [93], electrochemical devices fabrication [94], batteries, and hydrogen storage due to its excellent chemical and physical properties. To enhance the capacitance property and cyclic stability, MoS2 is combined with GQDs. MoS2 is considered as a potential supercapacitive electrode material due to its high surface area, intrinsic high- capacitance, and good coupling ability with conductive [91]. The specific capacitance of GQDs-MoS2 was showed 380 F g −1 under the current density of 0.6 A g −1. Furthermore, the thin film of MoS2-GQDs showed a much higher energy density of 38.47 Wh kg−1 at the current density 0.6 A g −1.

A new composite material by Zheng et al. is based on amino-functionalized GQDs and Ni(OH)2 (Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs) for the development of supercapacitors [31]. The preparation of electrode is shown in Fig. 10 . The af-GQDs, exfoliated graphene (EG) were prepared by an electrochemical process and Ni(OH)2 nanosheets were prepared by hydrothermal process. After that af-GQDs were mixed with Ni(OH)2 and prepare Ni(OH)2/af-GQDs. The surface of layered nanosheets of Ni(OH)2 is decorated by amino-functionalized GQDs to develop nanoporous (diameter <1.8 nm) sandwiched type architecture. The random aggregation in Ni(OH)2 nanosheets were decreased by the involvement of amino groups from af-GQDs. It also accelerates the ion transporting property of Ni(OH)2 nanosheets. Besides, the incorporation of af-GQDs leads to increase the cycling stability as well as specific capacitance of electrochemical double-layer (2653 F g −1 at 1 A g −1).

The development of electrochemical supercapacitors based on GQDs is promising for devices for improved energy storage and its conversion. These have been shown to have specific surface area, fast electron transportation property, increased ion diffusion rate and enhanced electrochemical performance. The bulk graphene does not have a suitable energy gap that limits its further applications. However, unlike, bulk graphene, GQDs have quantum confinement and edge effect, which alters its band gap, that increases the specific surface area, enhances the mobility of ions and enlarges its application in the energy storage field.

Although in recent years a number of advancements have been made on GQDs based supercapacitors, there are still many challenges. One of the critical challenges is that the size range of GQDs is not understandable. These problems create uncertainty in understanding the actual physiochemical properties of GQDs. Furthermore, its low conductivity, poor stability, lack of porosity, and unsymmetrical redox sites limit its application in the electrochemical field. Thus, the modification of GQDs is necessary to enhance its electrochemical performance. The incorporation of nanomaterials or doping by some heteroatoms in GQDs enhances the capacitive property due to the addition of exceptional optoelectronic properties, the luminescence characteristic, and bandgap tenability of the above materials. Therefore, in this article, the development of supercapacitors by GQDs based composite materials has been discussed. These materials are incorporated in GQDs for enhancing the capacitive performance. In particular, metal oxides (MnO2, RuO2, and NiO) metal nanoparticle, carbonaceous material (graphene oxide (GO), reduced graphene oxide (rGO), GQDs, carbon nanotube (CNTs), and carbon fibre), conducting polymers (polypyrrole, polyaniline, and polythiophene) are emerging to meet its actual requirements. Generally, the incorporation of such materials in GQDs was carried out by simple electrochemical deposition technique or hydrothermal treatment. The composition of such materials with GQDs provided a large surface area, fast charging/discharging rate, excellent electrochemical performance, and high cyclic stability, which are the basic criteria of an energy storage system.

Although GQDs based composite materials and their applications have introduced a significant improvement in recent years, challenges such as, i) for the fabrication of GQDs based supercapacitor as energy storage device, the bulk amount of electrode material is required. But yield of such materials with uniform size and shape are very limited. Some synthetic approaches have already been reported on the large scale production of GQDs [95,96]. amongst them, the use of green synthetic technique is very popular. But, the use of green raw materials or develop some green technology with control the reaction parameters are still at its early stage. It is difficult to say which parameter or technology is favoured for the synthesis of large scale production of GQDs. To our mind, the process based on microchannels devices is suitable for continuous production of GQDs. Second, the doping or surface functionalization with electron donating hetero atoms enhances the conjugation, resulting, the energy gap become narrower. It may also help to enhance the overall yield and improve the property of GQDs. Another method such as laser ablation in liquid produce GQDs with good conductivity and high yield may also fulfil the demand of bulk amount requirement. ii) The actual size of GQDs is not identified. As a result, the tiny difference in the size of GQDs may alter the physical and chemical properties of the composite materials. The productions of such materials are limited up to lab research only. The inability to industrialize such materials hinders lowering of the development cost of GQDs based composite materials, which seriously limits its practical application. The production of such materials by the green techniques will attract more attention in the future. It will provide pollution-free, less toxicity, and less energy consumption and reduces the difficulties in supercapacitor device fabrication. The another way to maintain the uniform size of GQDs is Digestive ripening (DR), or post-synthesis size modification method, in which polydisperse nanocrystal covert into almost monodisperse ones without resorting to any size separation. In general, there are three main steps are involved. In the first step, nanocrystals are synthesized by using chemical methods. In the second step of DR, to these nanocyrstal, a measuring amount of surface-active ligand, such as, amine, thiol, carboxylic acids etc. are added. After that ligand capped nanocrystal are separated from the reaction mixture by precipitation method. In the third and final step, these separated ligand-capped nanocrystal are again dispersed and refluxed it by adding excess of ligand. Interestingly, the obtained nanocrytals at the end of this simple and suitable procedure are in uniform and narrow size distributions range.

Therefore, further work is required to be done to develop such green techniques for efficient and low-cost industrial production to meet the growing energy demand. Explanation of the parameters concerned with the synthesis and properties of GQDs based composite materials, to enhance the performance of the supercapacitors is another area for further exploration.
